chunk_summary: This section outlines age-specific red flags and disposition tiers for undernutrition screening, emphasizing physiological and complication-based criteria for infants, children, and adolescents to guide immediate, urgent, or planned interventions.
learning_objectives: Understand the three-tier disposition framework for malnutrition screening results.
Identify age-specific red flags for severe acute malnutrition (SAM) and complications.
Learn criteria for outpatient eligibility and referral decisions based on medical stability and appetite.
Recognize signs that necessitate shifting focus from nutrition-only to targeted disease evaluation.
key_facts: Infants under 6 months: Tier 1 disposition for IMNCI danger signs, bilateral pitting oedema, hypoglycemia, hypothermia, severe dehydration, or inability to feed effectively.
Children 6–59 months: Tier 1 disposition for nutritional oedema, visible severe wasting, or complicated SAM features like lethargy, shock, or severe dehydration.
Children and adolescents 5–19 years: Severe thinness with hemodynamic instability, syncope, dehydration, or suspected severe anemia requires Tier 1 escalation.
Persistent fever or cough lasting ≥2 weeks, documented weight loss, or failure to gain weight are red flags for targeted disease evaluation.
Outpatient eligibility for SAM management depends on appetite and medical stability; uncertainty mandates referral as complicated SAM.
reference_quote: Infants under six months: tier 1 is indicated for any IMNCI general danger sign, bilateral pitting oedema, suspected sepsis, hypoglycemia, hypothermia, severe dehydration, convulsions, or inability to feed effectively. (IMNCI Chart Booklet for Medical Officers, 2023)
document_key_topics: Criteria for outpatient versus referral management
Integration of disease evaluation in malnutrition pathways
